match_info.result: LSG won by 3 wickets (with 0 balls remaining)

turning_points.label: Turning Point 1 — The Middle-Order Collapse
turning_points.description: LSG needed 126 off 72 balls at a point where the asking rate was manageable (10.5 RPO). Instead, Narine and Roy throttled the innings. Five wickets in eight overs. Pant fell for 10 to Roy. Pooran made 13 off 15 — barely above run-a-ball. By over 16, Narine had bowled all his overs and taken his 13-run toll. The middle overs were a squeeze — and KKR looked on course for their first win.

turning_points.label: Turning Point 2 — The Choudhary Arrival
turning_points.description: Badoni struck 21 off just 10 balls in the Choudhary/Badoni partnership to give the final assault legs. With Choudhary calibrating at the non-striker's end, Badoni went berserk against Roy. Two sixes in the 16th over changed the tempo. Then Badoni was caught at long-off off Roy — 139/6 — and for the third time in the chase, LSG looked beaten.

turning_points.label: Turning Point 3 — That 19th Over
turning_points.description: Arora bowled the final over of the match. He needed to defend 11. Choudhary hit him for six first ball (19.2), was held to dots in balls 3-4, then detonated another six off ball 5 to tie the match (19.5). The last delivery was a wide — a gift — and Avesh stole the bye that won the game off the very final legal delivery.

counterfactual_scenarios.title: What If Narine Had Been Average?
counterfactual_scenarios.change: Sunil Narine bowls at league average economy (8.5 RPO) instead of his actual 3.25 across 4 overs
counterfactual_scenarios.original_outcome: LSG won by 3 wickets off the last ball
counterfactual_scenarios.counterfactual_outcome: Chase becomes 203 — probability of LSG winning drops to ~25%
counterfactual_scenarios.narrative:
Narine returned to the KKR XI after recovering from illness, and he was immediately the tightest bowler on the park. In four overs he conceded just 13 runs — an economy of 3.25 in an IPL death-and-middle overs contest. That is not T20 bowling. That is something else. But what if he had merely been good rather than transcendent? At the league average of 8.5 RPO, he would have given up 34 runs. LSG's target balloons from 182 to 203 — a number that no last-ball finish can rescue.

In this counterfactual world, Mukul Choudhary's 54* off 27 balls — astonishing as it is — isn't enough. He needs 12 off the last over to win instead of 11. The mathematics shifts just enough to tip the game. Arora, who bowled the final over in reality, faces a batter who must hit a boundary every 2 balls rather than every 3.

Narine won the KKR this game within the game. That his team still lost is the cruelest cricket paradox — the best bowler on the field playing for the losing side.

counterfactual_scenarios.title: What If Choudhary Had Walked In Earlier?
counterfactual_scenarios.change: Choudhary bats at No.4 instead of No.6 — enters during the Pooran/Badoni partnership at over 12
counterfactual_scenarios.original_outcome: Choudhary entered at 118/5 in over 16 with 30 balls left
counterfactual_scenarios.counterfactual_outcome: With 48 balls to play with, LSG win by 6 wickets and 14 balls to spare
counterfactual_scenarios.narrative:
The match hinged on the toss within a toss: Rishabh Pant's batting order decision. Choudhary came in at No.6, entering at 118/5 in over 16 with the score at a knife-edge. Had Pant promoted him to No.4 — after Marsh fell at 56/2 — Choudhary would have had 48 balls to play with rather than 27.

In this version, the match isn't close. Choudhary and Badoni together for 60+ balls is a partnership that no T20 attack can sustain against. Pant's decision to send Pooran at 4 and trust the orthodox batting order very nearly cost LSG the game. That it didn't is entirely a function of Choudhary's extraordinary ability to compress a match into a handful of deliveries.

The number that stings for KKR is 14 — that is how many extra balls LSG would have consumed. Fourteen more deliveries for Choudhary means Arora never bowls a last over that matters.

game_changers.name: Mukul Choudhary
game_changers.reason: Entered at 118/5 in the 16th over with 64 needed from 30 balls. In the worst crisis position of the innings — 5 wickets gone, required rate of 12.8 — Choudhary manufactured 7 sixes and 2 fours in a display that compressed an impossible match into routine. His death-over SR of 200 is the stat. The image is Powell diving at long-off as ball after ball sailed clean over him.

game_changers.name: Sunil Narine
game_changers.reason: Narine returned from illness and immediately constricted the chase. In a match where every other KKR bowler bled runs, Narine's 4 overs yielded just 13 — clamping LSG in the middle overs when momentum might have built earlier. The tragedy is that his excellence gave KKR an 85% win probability that Choudhary demolished anyway.

game_changers.name: Ayush Badoni
game_changers.key_stat: 54 off 34 balls | Impact Player | Anchor through 4 partnerships
game_changers.reason: Badoni came in as Impact Player and held the chase together while wickets tumbled around him. His 54 off 34 isn't the number that wins LSG this game — but it's the scaffolding that kept the chase alive long enough for Choudhary to arrive. When Badoni was dismissed in over 17 at 139/6, the match felt over. It wasn't.

game_changers.name: Anukul Roy
game_changers.reason: Roy's three wickets — including Badoni and Pant — gave KKR the platform to defend 182. He dismissed Badoni at the crucial moment (over 17, 139/6) which looked like it had sealed the match for KKR. The twist is that he then had to bowl the 18th over to Choudhary, who deposited two consecutive sixes into the stand.
